[Senescence](/wiki/Senescence "Senescence"), long-term excessive alcohol consumption, [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), and [cerebrospinal fluid leak](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid_leak "Cerebrospinal fluid leak")[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

(**SDH**) is a type of bleeding in which a [collection of blood](/wiki/Hematoma "Hematoma")—usually but not always associated with a [traumatic brain injury](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury")—gathers between the inner layer of the [dura mater](/wiki/Dura_mater "Dura mater") and the [arachnoid mater](/wiki/Arachnoid_mater "Arachnoid mater") of the [meninges](/wiki/Meninges "Meninges") surrounding the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"). It usually results from rips in [bridging veins](/wiki/Bridging_vein "Bridging vein") that cross the [subdural space](/wiki/Subdural_space "Subdural space").

Hematoma usually associated with traumatic brain injury

A **subdural hematoma** (**SDH**) is a type of bleeding in which a [collection of blood](/wiki/Hematoma "Hematoma")—usually but not always associated with a [traumatic brain injury](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury")—gathers between the inner layer of the [dura mater](/wiki/Dura_mater "Dura mater") and the [arachnoid mater](/wiki/Arachnoid_mater "Arachnoid mater") of the [meninges](/wiki/Meninges "Meninges") surrounding the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"). It usually results from rips in [bridging veins](/wiki/Bridging_vein "Bridging vein") that cross the [subdural space](/wiki/Subdural_space "Subdural space").

Subdural hematomas may cause an increase in the [pressure inside the skull](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure"), which in turn can cause compression of and damage to delicate brain tissue. Acute subdural hematomas are often life-threatening. Chronic subdural hematomas have a better [prognosis](/wiki/Prognosis "Prognosis") if properly managed.

In contrast, [epidural hematomas](/wiki/Epidural_hematoma "Epidural hematoma") are usually caused by rips in [arteries](/wiki/Arteries "Arteries"), resulting in a build-up of blood between the dura mater and the [skull](/wiki/Skull "Skull"). The third type of brain hemorrhage, known as a [subarachnoid hemorrhage](/wiki/Subarachnoid_hemorrhage "Subarachnoid hemorrhage") (SAH), causes bleeding into the subarachnoid space between the arachnoid mater and the [pia mater](/wiki/Pia_mater "Pia mater"). SAHs are often seen in trauma settings or after rupture of intracranial aneurysms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Signs and symptoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Signs and symptoms")]

The symptoms of a subdural hematoma have a slower onset than those of epidural hematomas because the lower-pressure veins involved bleed more slowly than arteries. [Signs and symptoms](/wiki/Signs_and_symptoms "Signs and symptoms") of acute hematomas may appear in minutes, if not immediately,[[5]](#cite_note-5) but can also be delayed as much as two weeks.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Symptoms of chronic subdural hematomas are usually delayed more than three weeks after injury.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

If the bleeds are large enough to put pressure on the brain, signs of increased [intracranial pressure](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure") or brain damage will be present.[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3) Other symptoms of subdural hematoma can include any combination of the following:[[7]](#cite_note-7)

* Loss of [consciousness](/wiki/Consciousness "Consciousness") or fluctuating levels of consciousness
* Irritability
* [Seizures](/wiki/Seizures "Seizures")
* Pain
* [Numbness](/wiki/Numbness "Numbness")
* [Headache](/wiki/Headache "Headache") (either constant or fluctuating)
* [Dizziness](/wiki/Dizziness "Dizziness")
* [Disorientation](/wiki/Disorientation "Disorientation")
* [Amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia")
* Weakness or [lethargy](/wiki/Lethargy "Lethargy")
* [Nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea") or [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting")
* Loss of appetite
* Personality changes
* [Inability to speak](/wiki/Aphasia "Aphasia") or [slurred speech](/wiki/Dysarthria "Dysarthria")
* [Ataxia](/wiki/Ataxia "Ataxia"), or difficulty walking
* Loss of muscle control
* Altered breathing patterns
* Hearing loss or ringing in the ears ([tinnitus](/wiki/Tinnitus "Tinnitus"))
* Blurred vision
* [Deviated gaze](/wiki/Deviated_gaze "Deviated gaze"), or abnormal movement of the eyes.[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3)

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Causes")]

Subdural hematomas are most often caused by [head injury](/wiki/Head_injury "Head injury"), in which rapidly changing velocities within the [skull](/wiki/Human_skull "Human skull") may stretch and tear small [bridging veins](/wiki/Bridging_vein "Bridging vein"). Much more common than [epidural hemorrhages](/wiki/Epidural_hemorrhage "Epidural hemorrhage"), subdural hemorrhages generally result from [shearing injuries](/wiki/Shearing_injury "Shearing injury") due to various rotational or linear forces.[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3)[[2]](#cite_note-UVT-2) There are claims that they can occur in cases of [shaken baby syndrome](/wiki/Shaken_baby_syndrome "Shaken baby syndrome"), although there is no scientific evidence for this.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)

They are also commonly seen in the elderly and in people with an [alcohol use disorder](/wiki/Alcohol_use_disorder "Alcohol use disorder") who have evidence of [cerebral atrophy](/wiki/Cerebral_atrophy "Cerebral atrophy").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Cerebral atrophy increases the length the bridging veins have to traverse between the two meningeal layers, thus increasing the likelihood of shearing forces causing a tear.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is also more common in patients on [anticoagulants](/wiki/Anticoagulant "Anticoagulant") or [antiplatelet medications](/wiki/Antiplatelet_drug "Antiplatelet drug"), such as [warfarin](/wiki/Warfarin "Warfarin") and [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin"), respectively.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) People on these medications can have a subdural hematoma after a relatively minor traumatic event. Another cause can be a reduction in [cerebrospinal fluid](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid "Cerebrospinal fluid") pressure, which can reduce pressure in the subarachnoid space, pulling the arachnoid away from the dura mater and leading to a rupture of the blood vessels.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Risk factors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Risk factors")]

Factors increasing the risk of a subdural hematoma include being very young or very old [age](/wiki/Senescence "Senescence"). As the brain shrinks with age, the [subdural space](/wiki/Subdural_space "Subdural space") enlarges and the [veins](/wiki/Vein "Vein") that traverse the space must cover a wider distance, making them more vulnerable to tears. The elderly also have more brittle veins, making chronic subdural bleeds more common.[[11]](#cite_note-Downie-11) Infants, too, have larger subdural spaces and are more predisposed to subdural bleeds than are young adults.[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3) It is often claimed that subdural hematoma is a common finding in shaken baby syndrome, although there is no science to support this.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) In juveniles, an [arachnoid cyst](/wiki/Arachnoid_cyst "Arachnoid cyst") is a risk factor for subdural hematoma.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Other risk factors include taking blood thinners (anticoagulants), long-term [excessive alcohol consumption](/wiki/Alcohol_use_disorder "Alcohol use disorder"), [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), and [cerebrospinal fluid leaks](/wiki/Cerebrospinal_fluid_leak "Cerebrospinal fluid leak").[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)

## Pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pathophysiology")]

### Acute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Acute")]

Acute subdural hematoma is usually caused by external trauma that creates tension in the wall of a bridging vein as it passes between the arachnoid and dural layers of the brain's lining—i.e., the subdural space. The circumferential arrangement of [collagen](/wiki/Collagen "Collagen") surrounding the vein makes it susceptible to such tearing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Intracerebral hemorrhage](/wiki/Intracerebral_hemorrhage "Intracerebral hemorrhage") and ruptured cortical vessels (blood vessels on the surface of the brain) can also cause subdural hematoma. In these cases, blood usually accumulates between the two layers of the dura mater. This can cause ischemic brain damage by two mechanisms: one, pressure on the cortical blood vessels,[[13]](#cite_note-JNS_Acute-13) and two, [vasoconstriction](/wiki/Vasoconstriction "Vasoconstriction") due to the substances released from the hematoma, which causes further [ischemia](/wiki/Ischemia "Ischemia") by restricting blood flow to the brain.[[14]](#cite_note-graham-14) When the brain is denied adequate blood flow, a [biochemical cascade](/wiki/Biochemical_cascade "Biochemical cascade") known as the [ischemic cascade](/wiki/Ischemic_cascade "Ischemic cascade") is unleashed, and may ultimately lead to brain [cell death](/wiki/Cell_death "Cell death").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Subdural hematomas grow continually larger as a result of the pressure they place on the brain: As [intracranial pressure](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure") rises, blood is squeezed into the [dural venous sinuses](/wiki/Dural_venous_sinuses "Dural venous sinuses"), raising the dural venous pressure and resulting in more bleeding from the ruptured bridging veins. They stop growing only when the pressure of the hematoma equalizes with the intracranial pressure, as the space for expansion shrinks.[[13]](#cite_note-JNS_Acute-13)

### Chronic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chronic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Subdural_hematoma_-_very_low_mag.jpg/250px-Subdural_hematoma_-_very_low_mag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Subdural_hematoma_-_very_low_mag.jpg)

[Micrograph](/wiki/Micrograph "Micrograph") of a chronic subdural hematoma, as demonstrated by thin strands of collagen and neovascularization. [HPS stain](/wiki/HPS_stain "HPS stain")

In chronic subdural hematomas, blood accumulates in the dural space as a result of damage to the dural border cells.[[16]](#cite_note-Pathophysiology_of_CSDH-16) The resulting [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation") leads to new membrane formation through [fibrosis](/wiki/Fibrosis "Fibrosis") and produces fragile and leaky blood vessels through [angiogenesis](/wiki/Angiogenesis "Angiogenesis"), permitting the leakage of [red blood cells](/wiki/Red_blood_cell "Red blood cell"), [white blood cells](/wiki/White_blood_cell "White blood cell"), and [plasma](/wiki/Blood_plasma "Blood plasma") into the hematoma cavity. Traumatic tearing of the [arachnoid mater](/wiki/Arachnoid_mater "Arachnoid mater") also causes leakage of cerebrospinal fluid into the hematoma cavity, increasing the size of the hematoma over time. Excessive [fibrinolysis](/wiki/Fibrinolysis "Fibrinolysis") also causes continuous bleeding.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Pro-inflammatory mediators active in the hematoma expansion process include Interleukin 1α ([IL1A](/wiki/IL1A "IL1A")), [Interleukin 6](/wiki/Interleukin_6 "Interleukin 6"), and [Interleukin 8](/wiki/Interleukin_8 "Interleukin 8"), while the anti-inflammatory mediator is [Interleukin 10](/wiki/Interleukin_10 "Interleukin 10"). Mediators that promote angiogenesis are [angiopoietin](/wiki/Angiopoietin "Angiopoietin") and [vascular endothelial growth factor](/wiki/Vascular_endothelial_growth_factor "Vascular endothelial growth factor") (VEGF). [Prostaglandin E2](/wiki/Prostaglandin_E2 "Prostaglandin E2") promotes the expression of VEGF. [Matrix metalloproteinases](/wiki/Matrix_metalloproteinase "Matrix metalloproteinase") remove surrounding collagen, providing space for new blood vessels to grow.[[16]](#cite_note-Pathophysiology_of_CSDH-16)

[Craniotomy](/wiki/Craniotomy "Craniotomy") for unruptured [intracranial aneurysm](/wiki/Intracranial_aneurysm "Intracranial aneurysm") is another risk factor for the development of chronic subdural hematoma. The incision in the arachnoid membrane during the operation causes cerebrospinal fluid to leak into the subdural space, leading to inflammation. This complication usually resolves on its own.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Ct-scan_of_the_brain_with_an_subdural_hematoma.jpg/250px-Ct-scan_of_the_brain_with_an_subdural_hematoma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ct-scan_of_the_brain_with_an_subdural_hematoma.jpg)

A subdural hematoma demonstrated by CT

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/ChronicSubduralPostBurrHoles.jpg/250px-ChronicSubduralPostBurrHoles.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChronicSubduralPostBurrHoles.jpg)

Chronic subdural after treatment with [burr holes](/wiki/Burr_hole "Burr hole")

A person must receive medical assessment, including a complete [neurological](/wiki/Neurology "Neurology") examination, after any head trauma. A [CT scan](/wiki/CT_scan "CT scan") or [MRI scan](/wiki/MRI_scan "MRI scan") will usually detect significant subdural hematomas.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Subdural hematomas occur most often around the tops and sides of the [frontal](/wiki/Frontal_lobe "Frontal lobe") and [parietal lobes](/wiki/Parietal_lobe "Parietal lobe").[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3)[[2]](#cite_note-UVT-2) They also occur in the [posterior cranial fossa](/wiki/Posterior_cranial_fossa "Posterior cranial fossa"), and near the [falx cerebri](/wiki/Falx_cerebri "Falx cerebri") and [tentorium cerebelli](/wiki/Tentorium_cerebelli "Tentorium cerebelli").[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3) Unlike epidural hematomas, which cannot expand past the [sutures of the skull](/wiki/Skull_suture "Skull suture"), subdural hematomas can expand along the inside of the skull, creating a concave shape that follows the curve of the brain, stopping only at [dural reflections](/wiki/Dural_reflection "Dural reflection") like the tentorium cerebelli and falx cerebri.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On a CT scan, subdural hematomas are classically crescent-shaped, with a concave surface away from the skull. However, they can have a convex appearance, especially in the early stages of bleeding. This may cause difficulty in distinguishing between subdural and epidural hemorrhages. A more reliable indicator of subdural hemorrhage is its involvement of a larger portion of the cerebral hemisphere. Subdural blood can also be seen as a layering density along the tentorium cerebelli. This can be a chronic, stable process, since the feeding system is low-pressure. In such cases, subtle signs of bleeding—such as effacement of [sulci](/wiki/Sulcus_(neuroanatomy) "Sulcus (neuroanatomy)") or medial displacement of the junction between [gray matter](/wiki/Gray_matter "Gray matter") and [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter")—may be apparent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

| Age | Attenuation ([HU](/wiki/Hounsfield_units "Hounsfield units")) |
| --- | --- |
| First hours | +75 to +100[[18]](#cite_note-Rao2016-18) |
| After 3 days | +65 to +85[[18]](#cite_note-Rao2016-18) |
| After 10–14 days | +35 to +40[[19]](#cite_note-19) |

Fresh subdural bleeding is [hyperdense](/wiki/Radiodensity "Radiodensity"), but becomes more hypodense over time due to dissolution of cellular elements. After 3–14 days, the bleeding becomes isodense with brain tissue and may therefore be missed.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Subsequently, it will become more hypodense than brain tissue.[[21]](#cite_note-Schweitzer_2019-21)

### Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Classification")]

Subdural hematomas are classified as [acute](/wiki/Acute_(medicine) "Acute (medicine)"), subacute, or [chronic](/wiki/Chronic_(medicine) "Chronic (medicine)"), depending on the speed of their onset.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Acute bleeds often develop after high-speed acceleration or deceleration injuries. They are most severe if associated with [cerebral contusions](/wiki/Cerebral_contusion "Cerebral contusion").[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3) Though much faster than chronic subdural bleeds, acute subdural bleeding is usually venous and therefore slower than the arterial bleeding of an epidural hemorrhage. Acute subdural hematomas due to trauma are the most lethal of all head injuries and have a high [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") if they are not rapidly treated with surgical decompression.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The mortality rate is higher than that of epidural hematomas and [diffuse brain injuries](/wiki/Focal_and_diffuse_brain_injury "Focal and diffuse brain injury") because the force required to cause subdural hematomas tends to cause other severe injuries as well.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Chronic subdural bleeds develop over days to weeks, often after minor head trauma, though a cause is not identifiable in 50% of patients.[[11]](#cite_note-Downie-11) They may not be discovered until they present clinically months or years after a head injury.[[25]](#cite_note-Kushner98-25) The bleeding from a chronic hematoma is slow and usually stops by itself.[[2]](#cite_note-UVT-2)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Because these hematomas progress slowly, they can more often be stopped before they cause significant damage, especially if they are less than a centimeter wide. In one study, only 22% of patients with chronic subdural bleeds had outcomes worse than "good" or "complete recovery".[[3]](#cite_note-wagner-3) Chronic subdural hematomas are common in the elderly.[[25]](#cite_note-Kushner98-25)

## Treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Treatment")]

Treatment of a subdural hematoma depends on its size and rate of growth. Some small subdural hematomas can be managed by careful monitoring as the [blood clot](/wiki/Thrombus "Thrombus") is eventually resorbed naturally. Others can be treated by inserting a small [catheter](/wiki/Catheter "Catheter") through a hole drilled through the skull and sucking out the hematoma.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Large or symptomatic hematomas require a [craniotomy](/wiki/Craniotomy "Craniotomy"). A surgeon opens the skull and then the dura mater; removes the clot with suction or irrigation; and identifies and controls sites of bleeding.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) The injured vessels must be repaired. Postoperative complications can include increased [intracranial pressure](/wiki/Intracranial_pressure "Intracranial pressure"), brain [edema](/wiki/Edema "Edema"), new or recurrent bleeding, [infection](/wiki/Infection "Infection"), and [seizures](/wiki/Epileptic_seizure "Epileptic seizure"). In patients with a chronic subdural hematoma but no history of seizures, it is unclear whether [anticonvulsants](/wiki/Anticonvulsant "Anticonvulsant") are harmful or beneficial.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Those with chronic subudural haematoma (CSDH) with few or no symptoms or have high risk of complication during surgery may be treated conservatively with medications such as atorvastatin, dexamethasone,[[31]](#cite_note-pmid26342776-31) and mannitol, although supporting conservative treatment is still weak.[[32]](#cite_note-pmid28102879-32) [HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor](/wiki/HMG-CoA_reductase_inhibitor "HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor") such as [Atorvastatin](/wiki/Atorvastatin "Atorvastatin") can reduce the haematoma volume and improving neurological function in eight weeks.[[33]](#cite_note-pmid30073290-33) HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor may also reduce risk of recurrences in CSDH.[[34]](#cite_note-pmid27695533-34) Dexamethasone, when used together with surgical drainage, may reduce the recurrence rate of subdural haematoma.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Even with surgical evacuation of chronic subdural haematoma, the recurrence rate is high, ranging from 7 to 20%.[[32]](#cite_note-pmid28102879-32)

## Prognosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subdural_hematoma&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Prognosis")]

Acute subdural hematomas have one of the highest mortality rates of all head injuries, with 50 to 90 percent of cases resulting in death, depending on the underlying brain injury. About 20 to 30 percent of patients recover brain function.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Higher [Glasgow Coma Scale](/wiki/Glasgow_Coma_Scale "Glasgow Coma Scale") score, younger age and responsive pupils are associated with better outcomes in acute subdural hematomas, while the time between the injury and the surgical evacuation, or the type of surgery, do not have a statistically significant impact on the outcomes.[[37]](#cite_note-Koç-37) Additionally, chronic subdural hematomas (CSDHs) have a relatively high mortality rate (up to 16.7% in patients over the age of 65); however, they have an even higher rate of recurrence (as mentioned in the previous section).[[38]](#cite_note-Puerto_Rico_Recurrence_Scale:_Predi-38) For the aforementioned reasons, researchers have developed predictive grading scales to identify patients at high risk of CSDH recurrence, one of which is the Puerto Rico Recurrence Scale developed by Mignucci-Jiménez et al.[[38]](#cite_note-Puerto_Rico_Recurrence_Scale:_Predi-38)
